title: B&H Rail Corporation

infobox.value: Originally [3 ft](/wiki/3_ft_gauge_railways "3 ft gauge railways") (914 mm) gauge

* [Albany Port Railroad](/wiki/Albany_Port_Railroad "Albany Port Railroad") * [Arcade and Attica Railroad](/wiki/Arcade_and_Attica_Railroad "Arcade and Attica Railroad") * B&H Rail Corporation * [Batten Kill Railroad](/wiki/Batten_Kill_Railroad "Batten Kill Railroad") * [Buffalo and Pittsburgh Railroad](/wiki/Buffalo_and_Pittsburgh_Railroad "Buffalo and Pittsburgh Railroad") * [Buffalo Southern Railroad](/wiki/Buffalo_Southern_Railroad "Buffalo Southern Railroad") * [Clarendon and Pittsford Railroad](/w/index.php?title=Clarendon_and_Pittsford_Railroad&action=edit&redlink=1 "Clarendon and Pittsford Railroad (page does not exist)") * [Canadian National](/wiki/Canadian_National "Canadian National")

([reporting mark](/wiki/Reporting_mark "Reporting mark") **BH**), formerly the **Bath & Hammondsport Railroad**, is a [Class III](/wiki/Class_III_railroad "Class III railroad") shortline [railroad](/wiki/Railroad "Railroad"). Initially the line served the communities of [Bath, New York](/wiki/Bath_(village),_New_York "Bath (village), New York") and [Hammondsport, New York](/wiki/Hammondsport,_New_York "Hammondsport, New York"). In Bath, the railroad connected with the [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad") and the [Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad](/wiki/Delaware,_Lackawanna_and_Western_Railroad "Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad"). In 1996, the railroad was leased by the [Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad](/wiki/Livonia,_Avon_and_Lakeville_Railroad "Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad").

in 2001, and later that year this company was renamed **B&H Rail Corporation** and assumed a long-term lease of [Norfolk Southern Railway](/wiki/Norfolk_Southern_Railway "Norfolk Southern Railway") trackage between [Wayland](/wiki/Wayland_(village),_New_York "Wayland (village), New York"), Bath and [Painted Post](/wiki/Painted_Post,_New_York "Painted Post, New York").[[5]](#cite_note-5) At this time, the LAL embarked on an aggressive track rehab program, replacing many ties. The track to Rheims remains out of service, but is not abandoned. Beyond Rheims to Hammondsport the rails have been lifted.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

B&H Rail Corporation

| Overview | |
| --- | --- |
| Headquarters | [Cohocton, New York](/wiki/Cohocton,_New_York "Cohocton, New York"), U.S. |
| [Reporting mark](/wiki/Reporting_mark "Reporting mark") | BH |
| Locale | [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") |
| Dates of operation | 1872–present |
| Technical | |
| [Track gauge](/wiki/Track_gauge "Track gauge") | 4 ft 8+1⁄2 in (1,435 mm) [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") |
| Previous gauge | Originally [3 ft](/wiki/3_ft_gauge_railways "3 ft gauge railways") (914 mm) gauge |

**B&H Rail Corporation** ([reporting mark](/wiki/Reporting_mark "Reporting mark") **BH**), formerly the **Bath & Hammondsport Railroad**, is a [Class III](/wiki/Class_III_railroad "Class III railroad") shortline [railroad](/wiki/Railroad "Railroad"). Initially the line served the communities of [Bath, New York](/wiki/Bath_(village),_New_York "Bath (village), New York") and [Hammondsport, New York](/wiki/Hammondsport,_New_York "Hammondsport, New York"). In Bath, the railroad connected with the [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad") and the [Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad](/wiki/Delaware,_Lackawanna_and_Western_Railroad "Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad"). In 1996, the railroad was leased by the [Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad](/wiki/Livonia,_Avon_and_Lakeville_Railroad "Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=B%26H_Rail_Corporation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Hammondsport_Station_-_June_2016.jpg/250px-Hammondsport_Station_-_June_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hammondsport_Station_-_June_2016.jpg)

Hammondsport station

It was chartered in 1872 as a [3 ft](/wiki/3_ft_gauge_railways "3 ft gauge railways") (914 mm) [narrow gauge](/wiki/Narrow_gauge_railway "Narrow gauge railway") railroad running from the [Erie](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad") and [Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad](/wiki/Delaware,_Lackawanna_and_Western_Railroad "Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad")'s interchange in [Bath](/wiki/Bath_(village),_New_York "Bath (village), New York") to [Hammondsport, New York](/wiki/Hammondsport,_New_York "Hammondsport, New York"). In Hammondsport, passengers could continue north via a [ferry](/wiki/Ferry "Ferry") on [Keuka Lake](/wiki/Keuka_Lake "Keuka Lake"). The railroad was [converted](/wiki/Gauge_conversion "Gauge conversion") to 4 ft 8+1⁄2 in (1,435 mm) [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") in 1889.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Erie Railroad control (1903-1935)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=B%26H_Rail_Corporation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Erie Railroad control (1903-1935)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Bath_and_Hammondsport_Railroad_-_System_Index_Map.jpg/250px-Bath_and_Hammondsport_Railroad_-_System_Index_Map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bath_and_Hammondsport_Railroad_-_System_Index_Map.jpg)

1917 map of the line

The line eventually came under control of the [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad") around 1903. Passenger service ended in 1917, when steamboat service on Keuka Lake was discontinued. Business continued as usual until a flood in 1935 damaged much of the railroad. Faced with having to rebuild miles of railroad, the management of the Erie Railroad instead began abandonment proceedings. Freight service was temporarily provided by trucks. Five local businessmen joined together to purchase the B&H from the Erie and return it to local control. The railroad was repaired and reopened with ceremonies on July 25, 1936.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Independence and Expansion (1936-1996)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=B%26H_Rail_Corporation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Independence and Expansion (1936-1996)")]

1960 saw the merger of the Erie and the [Delaware, Lackawanna and Western](/wiki/Delaware,_Lackawanna_and_Western "Delaware, Lackawanna and Western") railroads with the formation of the [Erie Lackawanna](/wiki/Erie_Lackawanna "Erie Lackawanna"). On April 1, 1976, the Erie Lackawanna became part of [Conrail](/wiki/Conrail "Conrail"). That same year, Conrail sold the DL&W line between Bath and [Wayland](/wiki/Wayland_(village),_New_York "Wayland (village), New York") to the [Steuben County](/wiki/Steuben_County,_New_York "Steuben County, New York") Industrial Development Agency. B&H became the designated operator of the branch to Wayland. Primary freight traffic for the railroad was wine and related products, lumber, and general commodities.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Among the wineries served was the Great Western winery, also known as [Pleasant Valley Wine Company](/wiki/Pleasant_Valley_Wine_Company "Pleasant Valley Wine Company"). The county operated the line with the nickname of "The Champagne Route", and passenger excursions were operated by the Champagne Railroad (CGNE). These excursions ended in 1994, and much of the track to Hammondsport was taken out of service once wine shipments from Hammondsport ceased. Service continued between Bath and Wayland on the former EL track. Freight interchange was maintained with Conrail at Bath.

## LA&L Acquisition (1996-Present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=B%26H_Rail_Corporation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: LA&L Acquisition (1996-Present)")]

In May 1996, shortline [Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad](/wiki/Livonia,_Avon_and_Lakeville_Railroad "Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad") (LAL) assumed operation[[4]](#cite_note-4) of the county-owned trackage, including the original B&H. These operations were transferred to the subsidiary **Cohocton Valley Railroad** in 2001, and later that year this company was renamed **B&H Rail Corporation** and assumed a long-term lease of [Norfolk Southern Railway](/wiki/Norfolk_Southern_Railway "Norfolk Southern Railway") trackage between [Wayland](/wiki/Wayland_(village),_New_York "Wayland (village), New York"), Bath and [Painted Post](/wiki/Painted_Post,_New_York "Painted Post, New York").[[5]](#cite_note-5) At this time, the LAL embarked on an aggressive track rehab program, replacing many ties. The track to Rheims remains out of service, but is not abandoned. Beyond Rheims to Hammondsport the rails have been lifted.

As of August 2008, the B&H operates regular service between [Cohocton](/wiki/Cohocton_(village),_New_York "Cohocton (village), New York") and Painted Post. About half of a mile of the Hammondsport branch line is being used as a siding. The original line, designated at the Hammondsport Running Track, continues to be maintained for potential future service. Some new construction took place on the line from Bath to Painted Post just past the junction where dirt embankments have been built up as if additional sidings were going to be added. The railroad's main office and locomotive facility are located in Cohocton. A second engine house was built in [Coopers Plains](/wiki/Coopers_Plains,_New_York "Coopers Plains, New York") in 2012.

## Historic rolling stock

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=B%26H_Rail_Corporation&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Historic rolling stock")]

The last B&H steam locomotive was #11, was built by [Alco](/wiki/American_Locomotive_Company "American Locomotive Company") at its [Cooke Locomotive and Machine Works](/wiki/Cooke_Locomotive_and_Machine_Works "Cooke Locomotive and Machine Works") in [Paterson, New Jersey](/wiki/Paterson,_New_Jersey "Paterson, New Jersey"), for a sugar railway in [Cuba](/wiki/Cuba "Cuba"), but the order was never fulfilled. It was sold to the [Narragansett Pier Railroad](/wiki/Narragansett_Pier_Railroad "Narragansett Pier Railroad"), before being sold to the Bath & Hammondsport. It was retired in 1949 and put into storage. The B&H sold #11 to Dr. Stanley A. Groman for display at his Rail City Railroad Museum in 1955. After Rail City closed, the locomotive was sold back to the Narragansett Pier Railroad in 1977 to be used in an excursion service which never began. In 1981 it was sold to the [Middletown and New Jersey Railroad](/wiki/Middletown_and_New_Jersey_Railroad "Middletown and New Jersey Railroad"), again for possible excursion use that never came to fruition. It was sold to the [Everett Railroad](/wiki/Everett_Railroad "Everett Railroad") in 2006, where it was restored and returned to service.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The B&H replaced steam with two [General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric") [44-ton switchers](/wiki/GE_44-ton_switcher "GE 44-ton switcher"). These were eventually replaced with two [ALCo S1s](/wiki/ALCO_S1 "ALCO S1"). One ALCo remains on the property and sees occasional use, as the other, a former [New York Central](/wiki/New_York_Central_Railroad "New York Central Railroad") unit, was acquired by the [Rochester & Genesee Valley Railroad Museum](/wiki/Rochester_%26_Genesee_Valley_Railroad_Museum "Rochester & Genesee Valley Railroad Museum") in March 2025.[[7]](#cite_note-7)
